The year **2006** saw a number of significant events in **radio broadcasting**.

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_in_English-language_radio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Events")]

* [Quad Cities' radio stations](/wiki/Media_in_the_Quad_Cities "Media in the Quad Cities") [WKBF](/wiki/WKBF_(AM) "WKBF (AM)") (1270 AM) and [WHTS](/wiki/WLKU "WLKU") (98.9 FM), both owned by Mercury Broadcasting but operated by a joint sales agreement with [Clear Channel Communications](/wiki/Clear_Channel_Communications "Clear Channel Communications"), are sold during the year. The sale of WKBF from Mercury to EMF Broadcasting is completed in late 2005, and in February the format switches from [contemporary hit radio](/wiki/Contemporary_hit_radio "Contemporary hit radio") (which had been formatted at the frequency since 1987) to formatting Christian music as WKLU.[[1]](#cite_note-1) WKBF, which had been broadcasting a progressive talk format, is sold to Quad Cities Media and switches to Christian talk in December.[[2]](#cite_note-2)
* January 3 – [The BJ Shea Morning Experience](/wiki/The_BJ_Shea_Morning_Experience "The BJ Shea Morning Experience") switches to [KISW](/wiki/KISW "KISW") in Seattle, Washington, from the former FM Talk (now country) station [KKWF](/wiki/KKWF "KKWF").
* January 21 – Kix Brooks, one half of the country music superstar duo [Brooks & Dunn](/wiki/Brooks_%26_Dunn "Brooks & Dunn"), takes over as host of the long-running "[American Country Countdown](/wiki/American_Country_Countdown "American Country Countdown")." He succeeds [Bob Kingsley](/wiki/Bob_Kingsley "Bob Kingsley"), who had left the program after 27 years a month earlier, and had since started a new program called "[Bob Kingsley's Country Top 40](/wiki/Bob_Kingsley%27s_Country_Top_40 "Bob Kingsley's Country Top 40")." Kingsley's last show was December 24, 2005, and radio personality Michael Jay had served as interim host until Brooks' debut.
* [Dial Global](/wiki/Dial_Global "Dial Global") takes over operations of the [Transtar](/wiki/Transtar_Radio_Networks "Transtar Radio Networks") 24-hour networks from [Westwood One](/wiki/Westwood_One_(1976%E2%80%932011) "Westwood One (1976–2011)").
* June 13 – VNU Media, the publishers of [Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)"), acquires [Radio & Records](/wiki/Radio_%26_Records "Radio & Records"). The following July 14 [Billboard Radio Monitor](/wiki/Billboard_Radio_Monitor "Billboard Radio Monitor") will cease publication, followed by Radio & Records three weeks later on August 4. On August 11 both trades are merged into a "newly relaunched" [R&R](/wiki/R%26R_(magazine) "R&R (magazine)").
* June 14 – [KKST](/wiki/KKST "KKST")/[KEDG](/wiki/KEDG "KEDG")/[Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria,_Louisiana "Alexandria, Louisiana"), Louisiana trade formatted to KKST as Urban leader as "Kiss 98.7" and KEDG as Adult contemporary leader as "Star 106.9".
* In September, [Mediabase](/wiki/Mediabase "Mediabase"), associated with the original R&R for 19 years until their final issue (as an independent trade), begin publishing their chart listings in [USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today") and in the following December debut their online website.
* November 7 – [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") radio airs the 15,000th episode of its (near) daily drama serial *[The Archers](/wiki/The_Archers "The Archers")*, begun in 1951.

## Debuts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_in_English-language_radio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Debuts")]

* The [Rhythmic Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Rhythmic_Adult_Contemporary "Rhythmic Adult Contemporary") branded *[MOViN](/wiki/Movin%27_(brand) "Movin' (brand)")* makes it debut on 1 May, at [KQMV](/wiki/KQMV "KQMV")/[Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), Washington.
* [Mike McConnell](/wiki/Mike_McConnell_(radio_personality) "Mike McConnell (radio personality)"), launched into national syndication after several years at [WLW](/wiki/WLW "WLW") on July 1.
* On August 17 KZLA/Los Angeles drops Country after 26 years and becomes the second station in the United States to pick up the Rhythmic AC "MOViN'" format as [KMVN](/wiki/KLLI_(FM) "KLLI (FM)"). The flip would leave Los Angeles without an FM Country station until March 2007 when [KKGO](/wiki/KKGO "KKGO") fills the void.
* [Nova M Radio](/wiki/Nova_M_Radio "Nova M Radio"), a progressive talk radio network featuring [Mike Malloy](/wiki/Mike_Malloy "Mike Malloy"). October 18.
* [Greenstone Media](/w/index.php?title=Greenstone_Media&action=edit&redlink=1 "Greenstone Media (page does not exist)"), a female-oriented [talk radio](/wiki/Talk_radio "Talk radio") network
* Kevin Barrett begins hosting a twice-weekly talk show on [Genesis Communications Network](/wiki/Genesis_Communications_Network "Genesis Communications Network") called *The Dynamic Duo*, and another talk show, hosted weekly on RBN, titled *Truth Jihad Radio*. The topic of both shows is mainly conspiracy theories surrounding the [September 11, 2001 attacks](/wiki/September_11,_2001_attacks "September 11, 2001 attacks").
* San Francisco/Oakland oldies station [KFRC](/wiki/KMVQ-FM "KMVQ-FM") flipped to [Rhythmic Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Rhythmic_Adult_Contemporary "Rhythmic Adult Contemporary") as "MOViN 99.7".
* November 9 – [WAQZ](/wiki/WAQZ "WAQZ") in [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), Ohio flips to [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") as [WYGY](/wiki/WYGY "WYGY") "97.3 The Wolf".
* October 2 – [KMVK](/wiki/KMVK "KMVK") in [Fort Worth, Texas](/wiki/Fort_Worth,_Texas "Fort Worth, Texas") (formerly KOAI) begins their [Rhythmic Adult Contemporary](/wiki/Rhythmic_Adult_Contemporary "Rhythmic Adult Contemporary") format as *MOViN 107.5*. This change has caused a lot of criticism to former KOAI listeners.
* December 30 – "Casey Kasem's American Top 40 – The 1970s", digitally remastered airings of selected "[American Top 40](/wiki/American_Top_40 "American Top 40")" shows from 1970 to 1979.

## Closings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_in_English-language_radio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Closings")]

* May – *[Unforgettable Favorites](/wiki/Unforgettable_Favorites "Unforgettable Favorites")* merged into [Timeless Classics](/wiki/Stardust_(radio_format) "Stardust (radio format)") by owner [ABC Radio](/wiki/Citadel_Media "Citadel Media").
* June 23 – *[The Phil Hendrie Show](/wiki/The_Phil_Hendrie_Show "The Phil Hendrie Show")*, host [Phil Hendrie](/wiki/Phil_Hendrie "Phil Hendrie") decided to retire from radio in an effort to pursue an acting career. Reruns continued on two stations until 2007.
* September 1 – [John Batchelor](/wiki/John_Batchelor "John Batchelor")'s last show on [ABC Radio Network](/wiki/ABC_Radio_Network "ABC Radio Network")
* December 5 – *[Springer on the Radio](/wiki/Springer_on_the_Radio "Springer on the Radio")*, hosted by [Jerry Springer](/wiki/Jerry_Springer "Jerry Springer") ends
* *[The Majority Report](/wiki/The_Majority_Report "The Majority Report")*. Originally one of [Air America Radio](/wiki/Air_America_Radio "Air America Radio")'s featured programs, the show ended a few days after [Janeane Garofalo](/wiki/Janeane_Garofalo "Janeane Garofalo") left the show and the network. Later restructuring of the schedule saw her co-host, [Sam Seder](/wiki/Sam_Seder "Sam Seder"), move to early mornings.
* October 2 – KOAI in [Fort Worth, Texas](/wiki/Fort_Worth,_Texas "Fort Worth, Texas") has ended their [Smooth Jazz](/wiki/Smooth_Jazz "Smooth Jazz") format. (see KMVK in Debuts section)

## Deaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_in_English-language_radio&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Deaths")]

* January 9 – [Jack Snow](/wiki/Jack_Snow_(American_football) "Jack Snow (American football)"), 62, former [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") player and radio announcer (complications from a [staph infection](/wiki/Staph_infection "Staph infection"))[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* February 1
  + [Dick Bass](/wiki/Dick_Bass "Dick Bass"), 68, American pro [football](/wiki/Football_(American) "Football (American)") player and radio analyst.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
  + [Dick Brooks](/wiki/Dick_Brooks "Dick Brooks"), 63, American [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") race car driver and radio broadcaster (heart attack)[[5]](#cite_note-5)
  + [Ernest Dudley](/wiki/Ernest_Dudley "Ernest Dudley"), 97, British novelist, journalist, screenwriter, actor, radio broadcaster.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* February 3
  + [Al Lewis](/wiki/Al_Lewis_(actor) "Al Lewis (actor)"), 82, American actor (Grandpa Munster on *[The Munsters](/wiki/The_Munsters "The Munsters")*), [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_(United_States) "Green Party (United States)") political candidate, restaurateur, and radio host.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* February 27 – [Linda Smith](/wiki/Linda_Smith_(comedian) "Linda Smith (comedian)"), 48, English comedian.
* March 1 – [Harry Browne](/wiki/Harry_Browne "Harry Browne"), 72, American [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarianism "Libertarianism") writer, politician, U.S. Presidential [candidate](/wiki/Candidate "Candidate") and radio talk show host.
* June 26 – [Stan Torgerson](/wiki/Stan_Torgerson "Stan Torgerson"), 82, radio announcer for [Ole Miss](/wiki/Ole_Miss "Ole Miss") football and basketball games.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* July 4 – [John Hinde](/wiki/John_Hinde_(broadcaster) "John Hinde (broadcaster)"), 92, Australian film reviewer and journalist.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* July 31 – [Paul Eells](/wiki/Paul_Eells "Paul Eells"), 70, voice of the [Arkansas Razorbacks](/wiki/Arkansas_Razorbacks "Arkansas Razorbacks") football and basketball for radio and television, car accident.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* August 2 – [Robert Eric Wone](/wiki/Murder_of_Robert_Eric_Wone "Murder of Robert Eric Wone"), 32, American general counsel to [Radio Free Asia](/wiki/Radio_Free_Asia "Radio Free Asia") (stabbing).[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* August 25 – [Ross Warneke](/wiki/Ross_Warneke "Ross Warneke"), 54, Australian television commentator and radio broadcaster (cancer).[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* September 6 – [Sir John Drummond](/wiki/John_Drummond_(arts_administrator) "John Drummond (arts administrator)"), 71, former controller of [BBC Radio 3](/wiki/BBC_Radio_3 "BBC Radio 3") and [The Proms](/wiki/The_Proms "The Proms").[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* September 26
  + [Ralph Story](/wiki/Ralph_Story "Ralph Story"), 86, American radio broadcaster and television show host (*[The $64,000 Challenge](/wiki/The_64,000_Dollar_Question "The 64,000 Dollar Question")*), [emphysema](/wiki/Emphysema "Emphysema").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
  + [Iva Toguri D'Aquino](/wiki/Iva_Toguri_D%27Aquino "Iva Toguri D'Aquino"), 90, Japanese American broadcaster
* September 28 – [George Balzer](/wiki/George_Balzer "George Balzer"), 91, wrote for [Jack Benny](/wiki/Jack_Benny "Jack Benny")'s radio and TV shows, natural causes.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* October 3 – [Gwen Meredith](/wiki/Gwen_Meredith "Gwen Meredith"), 98, Australian writer of all 5,795 episodes of the long-running radio serial *[Blue Hills](/wiki/Blue_Hills_(radio_serial) "Blue Hills (radio serial)")*, after heart trouble.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* October 5 – [Robert Dentith](/w/index.php?title=Robert_Dentith&action=edit&redlink=1 "Robert Dentith (page does not exist)"), 29, British radio presenter of "The Unsigned Show" on [Kerrang! Radio](/wiki/Kerrang!_Radio "Kerrang! Radio").[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* October 13 – [Bob Lassiter](/wiki/Bob_Lassiter "Bob Lassiter"), 61, American [talk radio](/wiki/Talk_radio "Talk radio") personality.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* October 16 – [Lister Sinclair](/wiki/Lister_Sinclair "Lister Sinclair"), 85, Canadian playwright and [CBC](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") broadcaster ([pulmonary embolism](/wiki/Pulmonary_embolism "Pulmonary embolism"))
* October 17 – [Christopher Glenn](/wiki/Christopher_Glenn "Christopher Glenn"), 68, American [CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News") radio and television news anchor, [liver cancer](/wiki/Hepatocellular_carcinoma "Hepatocellular carcinoma").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* October 20 – [Mary Gay Taylor](/w/index.php?title=Mary_Gay_Taylor&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mary Gay Taylor (page does not exist)"), 71, radio journalist for [WCBS-AM](/wiki/WCBS-AM "WCBS-AM").[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* October 21
  + [Pye Chamberlayne](/wiki/Pye_Chamberlayne "Pye Chamberlayne"), 68, American radio journalist (heart attack).[[22]](#cite_note-22)
  + [Paul Walters](/wiki/Paul_Walters "Paul Walters"), 59, [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") radio and TV producer.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* November 23 – [Nick Clarke](/wiki/Nick_Clarke "Nick Clarke"), 58, [BBC Radio 4](/wiki/BBC_Radio_4 "BBC Radio 4") presenter and journalist (cancer).[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* November 27
  + [Casey Coleman](/wiki/Casey_Coleman "Casey Coleman"), 55, [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") sports broadcaster who won 4 Emmy Awards ([pancreatic cancer](/wiki/Pancreatic_cancer "Pancreatic cancer")).[[25]](#cite_note-25)
  + [Alan "Fluff" Freeman](/wiki/Alan_Freeman "Alan Freeman"), 79, former [BBC Radio](/wiki/BBC_Radio "BBC Radio") DJ, [natural causes](/wiki/Natural_causes "Natural causes").[[26]](#cite_note-26)
  + [Larry Henderson](/wiki/Larry_Henderson "Larry Henderson"), 89, first regular broadcaster on [CBC's](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") *[The National](/wiki/CBC_News:_The_National "CBC News: The National")*, [natural causes](/wiki/Natural_causes "Natural causes").
* December 17 - [Scott Mateer](/w/index.php?title=Scott_Mateer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Scott Mateer (page does not exist)"), 46, American [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy "Grammy")-nominated songwriter and DJ (complications of [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes") and [high blood pressure](/wiki/High_blood_pressure "High blood pressure")).[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* December 18
  + [Mike Dickin](/wiki/Mike_Dickin "Mike Dickin"), 63, British [Talksport](/wiki/Talksport "Talksport") radio presenter (car accident).[[28]](#cite_note-28)
  + [Mavor Moore](/wiki/Mavor_Moore "Mavor Moore"), 87, Canadian writer, actor, radio & TV producer and founder of theatrical institutions.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* *date unknown*
  + [Steve Crosno](/w/index.php?title=Steve_Crosno&action=edit&redlink=1 "Steve Crosno (page does not exist)"), 66, longtime [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas") radio DJ whose career spanned nearly 50 years (kidney failure).[[30]](#cite_note-30)
  + [Jaye Michael Davis](/w/index.php?title=Jaye_Michael_Davis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jaye Michael Davis (page does not exist)"), 62, veteran U.S. radio [deejay](/wiki/Deejay "Deejay") (motorcycle accident).[[31]](#cite_note-31)
  + [George Edwards](/w/index.php?title=George_Edward_Steinhardt&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Edward Steinhardt (page does not exist)"), 87, American radio host for [WQXR](/wiki/WXNY-FM "WXNY-FM").[[32]](#cite_note-32)
  + [Len "Boom" Goldberg](/w/index.php?title=Len_%22Boom%22_Goldberg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Len \"Boom\" Goldberg (page does not exist)"), 74, longtime station voice and DJ for [WMMS](/wiki/WMMS "WMMS") in [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), Ohio, and the first station voice for New York City's [WHTZ](/wiki/WHTZ "WHTZ") "Z100" upon their 1983 launch, heart attack.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
  + [Dick Johnson](/w/index.php?title=Dick_Johnson_(journalist)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dick Johnson (journalist) (page does not exist)"), 69, veteran [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine") radio broadcaster and news reporter, complications of a heart attack.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
  + [Don Lunn](/w/index.php?title=Don_Lunn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Don Lunn (page does not exist)"), 72, Australian breakfast radio DJ.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
  + [Michael Vestey](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Vestey&action=edit&redlink=1 "Michael Vestey (page does not exist)"), 61, former [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") correspondent and radio critic on *[The Spectator](/wiki/The_Spectator "The Spectator")* magazine.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
  + [Neville Willoughby](/w/index.php?title=Neville_Willoughby&action=edit&redlink=1 "Neville Willoughby (page does not exist)"), 69, Jamaican radio broadcaster (car accident).[[37]](#cite_note-37)
